docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Math Kickstarter

Self-paced mathematics brush-up for engineering students at VIA University College

docs.title: About this site
docs.text:
Math Kickstarter is an online refresher course covering foundational mathematics topics. It is designed for students who need to strengthen their skills before — or during — their engineering programme.

This site is not a replacement for the full mathematics curriculum — it covers selected foundational topics through short video lectures, self-check quizzes, and exercises with solutions organised into five topic areas.

docs.title: Before you begin: the pre-test
docs.text:
Have you taken the pre-test?

Before using this site, you should complete the mathematics pre-test provided by your programme.

  • If you score 75% or above, you generally do not need to work through this material.
  • If you score below 75%, you should use this site to review the topics where you need support.

Your pre-test result will indicate which topic areas to focus on. Follow those recommendations rather than working through every topic from start to finish.

Take the pre-test here

docs.title: How to use this site
docs.text:
1. Take the pre-test and note which topic areas you should review.
  2. Open the relevant topic(s) from the Topics menu.
  3. Watch the videos in the order shown on each topic page.
  4. Take the quiz at the bottom of the topic page. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding of the material.
  5. Work through the exercises. Use the collapsible answers to check your work.

You can revisit any topic at any time. Quizzes are for self-assessment only — results are not stored or reported.

If you need several topics, we suggest starting with Basic Arithmetic and Equations and progressing through to Vectors in 2D — see the Topics overview for details.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text:
Questions about the course material, contact Assistant Professor Mette Bjerg, memo@via.dk.

Questions about the pre-test or this site, contact Associate Professor Richard Brooks, rib@via.dk.

Questions about your specific programme requirements, contact your programme coordinator.

docs.title: Index
docs.text:
Topics

Click a topic in the menu (or in the table below) to access videos, a self-check quiz, and exercises for that area. Work through the topics recommended to you after the pre-test, or review any topic you want to strengthen.

docs.title: What this course covers
docs.text:
Topic Subtopics Basic Arithmetic and Equations Order of operations, fractions, exponents and radicals, parentheses, equations, quadratic equations, rearranging formulae Functions Functions and graphs, common function types, inverse functions Differential Calculus Derivatives and tangents, differentiation rules Integral Calculus Indefinite integrals and antiderivatives, definite integrals and areas Vectors in 2D Vectors in 2D, dot product, parametric equation of a line

Some advanced techniques are deliberately excluded (for example the product, quotient, and chain rules for differentiation, and integration by substitution or partial fractions). The focus is on the core skills needed for engineering mathematics.

docs.title: Recommended order
docs.text:
If you need to work through several topics, we suggest the following order:

  1. Basic Arithmetic and Equations
  2. Functions
  3. Differential Calculus
  4. Integral Calculus
  5. Vectors in 2D

Within each topic, watch the videos in the order they appear on the page before taking the quiz and working on the exercises.

docs.title: Basic Arithmetic and Equations
docs.text:
Basic Arithmetic and Equations

Work through the videos below in order. When you have watched them, take the quiz and try the exercises. A quiz score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
After working through this topic, you should be able to:

  • Apply the correct order of operations in arithmetic expressions
  • Add, subtract, multiply, and divide fractions
  • Work with exponents and radicals
  • Expand and simplify expressions involving parentheses, including \((a+b)^2\)
  • Solve linear and simple quadratic equations
  • Rearrange formulae to make a different variable the subject

docs.title: 1. Order of operations
docs.text: Apply the order of operations (PEMDAS): parentheses, exponents, multiplication and division, then addition and subtraction.

docs.title: 2. Fractions
docs.text: Work with numerators and denominators, find common denominators, and add, multiply, and divide fractions.

docs.title: 3. Exponents and radicals
docs.text: Use the laws of exponents, including negative and zero powers, and simplify expressions with square roots.

docs.title: 4. Parentheses
docs.text: Expand brackets using the distributive law and recognise special products such as \((a+b)^2\).

docs.title: 5. Equations
docs.text: Understand what the equals sign means and solve linear equations by applying the same operation to both sides.

docs.title: 6. Quadratic equations
docs.text: Write quadratic equations in standard form, use the quadratic formula, and interpret the discriminant.

docs.title: 7. Rearranging formulae
docs.text: Make a different variable the subject of a formula by undoing operations in reverse order.

docs.title: Quiz
docs.text:
When you have been through the videos, test your understanding with the quiz below. The quiz contains 10 questions. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding. You can see your total score at the bottom of the page, as well as your score for each question. You can retake the quiz as many times as you like — results are not stored or assessed.

Question 1In the expression $\dfrac{3 + 2 \times 6}{5}$, which statement best describes how it should be evaluated?Add 3 and 2 first because addition comes before divisionTreat the numerator as having an implicit grouping: compute $3 + 2 \times 6$ before dividing by 5Divide 2 by 5 before multiplying by 6Evaluate the denominator before the numeratorCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: A fraction bar groups the numerator as a single expression. Inside the numerator, multiplication comes before addition, so $3 + 12 = 15$, and then $15 \div 5 = 3$.

Question 2What is the value of $6 - 2 \times 5^2$?$200$$-44$$16$$-16$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Exponentiation comes first: $5^2 = 25$. Then multiplication: $2 \times 25 = 50$. Finally subtraction: $6 - 50 = -44$.

Question 3Which step is **essential** when adding $\dfrac{5}{6}$ and $\dfrac{1}{4}$?Multiply the numerators directlyFind a common denominator before adding the numeratorsAdd the denominators and keep the larger numeratorConvert both fractions to decimals and roundCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Fractions can only be added directly when they share a denominator. Here the least common multiple of 6 and 4 is 12, giving $\dfrac{10}{12} + \dfrac{3}{12} = \dfrac{13}{12}$.

Question 4Which simplification is correct?$\dfrac{a^5}{a^2} = a^{10}$$\dfrac{a^5}{a^2} = a^3$$\dfrac{a^5}{a^2} = a^{2.5}$$\dfrac{a^5}{a^2} = 3a$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: When dividing powers with the same base, subtract the exponents: $a^{5-2} = a^3$.

Question 5Which expression is equal to $2^{-3}$?$-8$$\dfrac{1}{8}$$-6$$\dfrac{1}{6}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: A negative exponent means reciprocal: $2^{-3} = \dfrac{1}{2^3} = \dfrac{1}{8}$. It does not mean the base becomes negative.

Question 6Which expansion is correct?$2r - (3 - r) = 2r - 3 - r$$2r - (3 - r) = 3r - 3$$2r - (3 - r) = r - 3$$2r - (3 - r) = 2r - 3 + r - 3$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Subtracting a bracket means changing the sign of every term inside: $2r - 3 + r = 3r - 3$.

Question 7The equation $4x - 2 = 18 - x$ is solved by first adding $x$ to both sides. Why is this a valid first step?Because $x$ must always be moved to the left-hand sideBecause it collects all $x$-terms on one side while keeping the equation balancedBecause subtraction is always done before additionBecause the right-hand side is larger than the left-hand sideCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The goal is to isolate $x$. Adding $x$ to both sides gives $5x - 2 = 18$, which gathers the variable terms without changing the solution set.

Question 8Before applying the quadratic formula to $4x(x + 1) + 3 = 2$, what must you do first?Divide every term by 4Expand and simplify to write the equation in standard form $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$Take the square root of both sides immediatelySet $x = 0$ and stopCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The quadratic formula applies only after the equation is in standard form. Expanding gives $4x^2 + 4x + 1 = 0$.

Question 9For $2x^2 - 4x - 6 = 0$, what does the discriminant tell you?There are no real solutions because $b$ is negativeThere are two distinct real solutions because the discriminant is positiveThere is exactly one solution because the leading coefficient is 2The solutions must be fractions because $c$ is negativeCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Here $b^2 - 4ac = (-4)^2 - 4(2)(-6) = 16 + 48 = 64 > 0$, so there are two distinct real solutions.

Question 10To make $r$ the subject of $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$, which sequence of inverse operations is correct?Divide by $\pi$, then subtract $\dfrac{4}{3}$, then take the square rootDivide by $\dfrac{4}{3}\pi$, then take the cube rootMultiply by 3, then divide by 4, then squareTake the cube root first, then divide by $V$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Undo the operations in reverse order: first divide both sides by $\dfrac{4}{3}\pi$ to isolate $r^3$, then take the cube root to get $r = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3V}{4\pi}}$.

docs.title: Exercise 2
docs.text:
Simplify:

  1. \(\dfrac{5}{6} - \dfrac{1}{4}\)
  2. \(\dfrac{2}{3} \div \dfrac{5}{9}\)
  3. \(\dfrac{3}{4} \times \dfrac{8}{15} + \dfrac{1}{2}\)
  1. \(\dfrac{10}{12} - \dfrac{3}{12} = \dfrac{7}{12}\)
  2. \(\dfrac{2}{3} \times \dfrac{9}{5} = \dfrac{6}{5}\)
  3. \(\dfrac{24}{60} + \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{2}{5} + \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{9}{10}\)

docs.title: Exercise 5
docs.text:
Solve for \(x\):

  1. \(4x - 2 = 18 - x\)
  2. \(\dfrac{2z + 3}{2} = z + 4\)
  1. \(5x = 20 \Rightarrow x = 4\)
  2. \(2z + 3 = 2z + 8\), which simplifies to \(3 = 8\), so there is no solution

docs.title: Exercise 6
docs.text:
Solve the following quadratic equations:

  1. \(2x^2 - 4x - 6 = 0\)
  2. \(4x(x + 1) + 3 = 2\)
  1. \(x^2 - 2x - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow (x - 3)(x + 1) = 0\), so \(x = 3\) or \(x = -1\)
  2. \(4x^2 + 4x + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow (2x + 1)^2 = 0\), so \(x = -\dfrac{1}{2}\) (one repeated root)

docs.title: Exercise 7
docs.text:
For each equation, find the discriminant and state the number of real solutions:

  1. \(x^2 + 6x + 9 = 0\)
  2. \(3x^2 - 2x + 5 = 0\)
  1. \(\Delta = 36 - 36 = 0\), so one real solution
  2. \(\Delta = 4 - 60 = -56 < 0\), so no real solutions

docs.title: Exercise 8
docs.text:
Rearrange each formula to make the requested variable the subject:

  1. \(V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3\) for \(r\)
  2. \(A = P(1 + r)^t\) for \(r\)
  1. \(r = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3V}{4\pi}}\)
  2. \(\dfrac{A}{P} = (1 + r)^t\), so \(1 + r = \left(\dfrac{A}{P}\right)^{1/t}\) and \(r = \left(\dfrac{A}{P}\right)^{1/t} - 1\)

docs.title: Exercise 10
docs.text:
Solve for \(x\):

\[\dfrac{3(x - 2)}{4} - \dfrac{2(x + 1)}{3} = 1\]

Show your working and verify the solution.

Multiply through by 12: \(9(x - 2) - 8(x + 1) = 12\)

\(9x - 18 - 8x - 8 = 12 \Rightarrow x = 38\)

Check: \(\dfrac{3(36)}{4} - \dfrac{2(39)}{3} = 27 - 26 = 1\) ✓

Total Score Summary

Your total score for all quizzes on this page is: 0 / 0

docs.title: Differential Calculus
docs.text:
Differential Calculus

Work through the videos below in order. When you have watched them, take the quiz and try the exercises. A quiz score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
After working through this topic, you should be able to:

  • Interpret the derivative as the slope of a tangent line
  • Differentiate simple power functions and common expressions
  • Apply basic differentiation rules (this topic does not cover product, quotient, or chain rule)

docs.title: 1. Derivatives and tangents
docs.text: Interpret the derivative as the slope of a tangent and as a rate of change.

docs.title: 2. Differentiation rules
docs.text: Differentiate sums, differences, and constant multiples, and use common derivative formulas.

docs.title: Quiz
docs.text:
When you have been through the videos, test your understanding with the quiz below. The quiz contains 10 questions. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding. You can see your total score at the bottom of the page, as well as your score for each question. You can retake the quiz as many times as you like — results are not stored or assessed.

Question 1What is the derivative of $f(x) = x^3$?$3x$$3x^2$$x^2$$3x^3$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Using the power rule, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^n) = nx^{n-1}$, so $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^3) = 3x^2$.

Question 2Geometrically, what does $f'(a)$ represent at a point on the graph of $f$?The $y$-coordinate of the point $(a, f(a))$The slope of the tangent line to the graph at $x = a$The area under the graph from $0$ to $a$The average slope of the graph over the interval $[0, a]$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The derivative at a point gives the instantaneous rate of change of $f$, which equals the slope of the tangent line at that point.

Question 3If $f'(x) > 0$ for all $x$ in an interval, what can we conclude about $f$ on that interval?$f$ is decreasing$f$ is increasing$f$ has a local maximum$f$ is constantCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: A positive derivative means the function's output is rising as $x$ increases, so $f$ is increasing on that interval.

Question 4At a local maximum of a differentiable function $f$, what must be true about $f'(x)$ at that point?$f'(x) = 1$$f'(x) = 0$$f'(x)$ is undefined$f'(x) < 0$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: At a local maximum the tangent line is horizontal, so the slope — and hence the derivative — is zero.

Question 5What is $\dfrac{d}{dx}(e^x)$?$xe^{x-1}$$e^x$$\dfrac{1}{x}$$e$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The exponential function $e^x$ is its own derivative: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(e^x) = e^x$.

Question 6What is $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\ln x)$?$e^x$$\dfrac{1}{x}$$x$$\ln x$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The derivative of the natural logarithm is $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\ln x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ (for $x > 0$).

Question 7What is $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x)$?$-\sin x$$\cos x$$-\cos x$$\sin x$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Differentiating sine gives cosine: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = \cos x$.

Question 8What is $\dfrac{d}{dx}(4x^2 - 3x + 7)$?$4x - 3$$8x - 3$$8x^2 - 3$$4x^2 - 3$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Apply the sum/difference and constant-multiple rules together with the power rule: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(4x^2) = 8x$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(-3x) = -3$, and the derivative of the constant $7$ is $0$.

Question 9Which of the following is **not** covered in this topic?The constant multiple ruleThe chain ruleThe power rule for $x^n$The derivative of $\cos x$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: This topic covers basic rules and common derivatives only. The product rule, quotient rule, and chain rule are left for later study.

Question 10Using the constant multiple rule, what is $\dfrac{d}{dx}(5\sin x)$?$5\cos x + C$$5\cos x$$\sin(5x)$$5\sin x$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The constant multiple rule gives $\dfrac{d}{dx}(5\sin x) = 5 \cdot \dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = 5\cos x$. Note that $+C$ belongs to integration, not differentiation.

docs.title: Exercise 1
docs.text:
Differentiate the following:

  1. \(f(x) = 5x^4\)
  2. \(f(x) = 3x^2 + 2x - 7\)
  1. \(f'(x) = 20x^3\)
  2. \(f'(x) = 6x + 2\)

docs.title: Exercise 3
docs.text:
Differentiate \(f(x) = e^x + 6\).

\(f'(x) = e^x\)

docs.title: Exercise 4
docs.text:
Differentiate \(f(x) = \ln x\).

\(f'(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}\)

docs.title: Exercise 5
docs.text:
Differentiate \(f(x) = \sin x + \cos x\).

\(f'(x) = \cos x - \sin x\)

docs.title: Exercise 6
docs.text:
Differentiate \(f(x) = 2\sin x - 3\cos x + e^x\).

\(f'(x) = 2\cos x + 3\sin x + e^x\)

docs.title: Exercise 7
docs.text:
Find the value(s) of \(x\) where \(f'(x) = 0\) for \(f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3\).

\(f'(x) = 2x - 4\), so \(2x - 4 = 0\) gives \(x = 2\).

docs.title: Exercise 8
docs.text:
For \(f(x) = -x^2 + 6x\), find \(f'(x)\) and state whether \(f\) is increasing or decreasing at \(x = 1\).

\(f'(x) = -2x + 6\). At \(x = 1\), \(f'(1) = 4 > 0\), so \(f\) is increasing at \(x = 1\).

docs.title: Exercise 10
docs.text:
The graph of \(f(x) = x^3 - 3x\) has a local maximum and a local minimum. Find where \(f'(x) = 0\) and state which value of \(x\) gives the local maximum.

\(f'(x) = 3x^2 - 3 = 3(x^2 - 1) = 3(x-1)(x+1)\), so \(f'(x) = 0\) at \(x = -1\) and \(x = 1\). Evaluating: \(f(-1) = 2\) and \(f(1) = -2\), so the local maximum occurs at \(x = -1\).

Total Score Summary

Your total score for all quizzes on this page is: 0 / 0

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
Functions

Work through the videos below in order. When you have watched them, take the quiz and try the exercises. A quiz score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
After working through this topic, you should be able to:

  • Explain domain, range, and the graph of a function
  • Recognise and work with linear, power, and exponential functions
  • Understand and find inverse functions where they exist

docs.title: 1. Functions and graphs
docs.text: See functions as input–output rules, identify domain and range, and read values from graphs.

docs.title: 2. Common function types
docs.text: Recognise linear, power, and exponential functions and how each one grows or changes.

docs.title: 3. Inverse functions
docs.text: Find inverse functions where they exist and connect exponentials with logarithms.

docs.title: Quiz
docs.text:
When you have been through the videos, test your understanding with the quiz below. The quiz contains 10 questions. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding. You can see your total score at the bottom of the page, as well as your score for each question. You can retake the quiz as many times as you like — results are not stored or assessed.

Question 1Which of the following best describes the domain of a function?The set of all output values of the functionThe set of all input values for which the function is definedThe set of points where the graph crosses the $x$-axisThe highest value the function can takeCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The domain is the set of all values that can be used as input to the function.

Question 2What is the range of $f(x) = x^2$?All real numbersAll real numbers greater than or equal to $0$All real numbers except $0$All integersCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Squaring any real number gives a result $\ge 0$, and every non-negative value is achieved (e.g. $f(0)=0$, $f(2)=4$). So the range is $[0, \infty)$.

Question 3If $f(x) = 3x - 4$, what is $f(5)$?$7$$11$$15$$-1$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Substitute $x = 5$: $f(5) = 3(5) - 4 = 15 - 4 = 11$.

Question 4Which of the following is a linear function?$f(x) = x^2 + 1$$f(x) = -2x + 7$$f(x) = 2^x$$f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: A linear function has the form $f(x) = ax + b$, where $a$ and $b$ are constants. Only $-2x + 7$ fits this form.

Question 5The function $f(x) = 5x^3$ is an example of which type?An exponential functionA power functionA linear functionA logarithmic functionCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: A power function has the form $f(x) = ax^n$, where the variable appears as a base raised to a fixed exponent. Here $f(x) = 5x^3$.

Question 6For $f(x) = 4 \cdot 3^x$, what are the initial value and the base?Initial value $3$, base $4$Initial value $4$, base $3$Initial value $4$, base $x$Initial value $1$, base $12$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: In $f(x) = b \cdot a^x$, $b$ is the initial value (when $x = 0$, $f(0) = b$) and $a$ is the base. So $b = 4$ and $a = 3$.

Question 7Under what condition does a function have an inverse function?It must be a linear functionIt must be one-to-one (each output comes from exactly one input)It must pass through the originIts domain must be all real numbersCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: An inverse exists only when the function is one-to-one. If two different inputs give the same output, you cannot uniquely reverse the mapping.

Question 8To find the inverse of $y = f(x)$ algebraically, which step comes first?Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$Swap $x$ and $y$ in the equationSquare both sidesReplace $x$ with $f^{-1}(x)$ immediatelyCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Finding an inverse means reversing input and output. Swapping $x$ and $y$ reflects this reversal before solving for the new output variable.

Question 9The graph of $f^{-1}$ is the reflection of the graph of $f$ across which line?The $x$-axisThe line $y = x$The $y$-axisThe line $y = -x$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Points $(a, b)$ on $f$ correspond to $(b, a)$ on $f^{-1}$. Swapping coordinates reflects the graph across the line $y = x$.

Question 10Which statement correctly links logarithms and exponentials?$\log_a(x)$ is the same as $a^x$$\log_a(x)$ is the inverse of $a^x$$\log_a(x) = x^a$$\log_a(x)$ only exists when $a = e$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: If $y = a^x$, then $x = \log_a(y)$. The logarithm undoes exponentiation, so $\log_a$ and $a^x$ are inverse functions (for $a > 0$, $a \ne 1$).

docs.title: Exercise 1
docs.text:
Find the domain and range of \(f(x) = \sqrt{x - 3}\).

Domain: \(x \ge 3\), i.e. \([3, \infty)\).

Range: \(f(x) \ge 0\), i.e. \([0, \infty)\).

docs.title: Exercise 3
docs.text:
Identify each function as linear, power, or exponential:

  1. \(f(x) = 7 - 3x\)
  2. \(h(x) = 2x^{0.5}\)
  3. \(p(x) = 100 \cdot 0.5^x\)
  1. Linear (\(f(x) = -3x + 7\))
  2. Power (\(h(x) = 2x^{1/2}\))
  3. Exponential (\(p(x) = 100 \cdot 0.5^x\))

docs.title: Exercise 4
docs.text:
Rearrange each formula to make the indicated variable the subject:

  1. \(V = IR\) — solve for \(I\)
  2. \(y = mx + c\) — solve for \(x\)
  3. \(P = \dfrac{E}{t}\) — solve for \(t\)
  1. \(I = \dfrac{V}{R}\)
  2. \(x = \dfrac{y - c}{m}\)
  3. \(t = \dfrac{E}{P}\)

docs.title: Exercise 6
docs.text:
Find the inverse of \(f(x) = 4x - 9\). State the domain and range of both \(f\) and \(f^{-1}\).

Swap \(x\) and \(y\): \(x = 4y - 9\)

Solve for \(y\): \(y = \dfrac{x + 9}{4}\)

So \(f^{-1}(x) = \dfrac{x + 9}{4}\)

Domain of \(f\): all real numbers; range of \(f\): all real numbers.

Domain of \(f^{-1}\): all real numbers; range of \(f^{-1}\): all real numbers.

docs.title: Exercise 7
docs.text:
The power dissipated in a circuit is \(P = I^2 R\). Rearrange to express \(I\) in terms of \(P\) and \(R\), then evaluate \(I\) when \(P = 50\,\text{W}\) and \(R = 2\,\Omega\).

\(I = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{R}}\)

\(I = \sqrt{\dfrac{50}{2}} = \sqrt{25} = 5\,\text{A}\)

docs.title: Exercise 9
docs.text:
Sketch (on paper) the graphs of \(f(x) = 2x + 1\) and its inverse on the same axes. What line of symmetry do they share?

\(f^{-1}(x) = \dfrac{x - 1}{2}\)

The graphs are reflections of each other across the line \(y = x\).

docs.title: Exercise 10
docs.text:
A population grows according to \(N(t) = 500 \cdot 2^t\), where \(t\) is time in years.

  1. What is the initial population?
  2. What is the population after 3 years?
  3. Rearrange the formula to express \(t\) in terms of \(N\) (use logarithms).
  1. Initial population: \(N(0) = 500 \cdot 2^0 = 500\)
  2. After 3 years: \(N(3) = 500 \cdot 2^3 = 500 \cdot 8 = 4000\)
  3. \(\dfrac{N}{500} = 2^t\), so \(t = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{N}{500}\right)\)
Total Score Summary

Your total score for all quizzes on this page is: 0 / 0

docs.title: Integral Calculus
docs.text:
Integral Calculus

Work through the videos below in order. When you have watched them, take the quiz and try the exercises. A quiz score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
After working through this topic, you should be able to:

  • Find indefinite integrals (antiderivatives) of simple functions
  • Evaluate definite integrals
  • Interpret definite integrals as area under a curve
  • Apply basic integration techniques only (no substitution or partial fractions)

docs.title: 1. Indefinite integrals and antiderivatives
docs.text: Find antiderivatives and evaluate basic indefinite integrals, including the constant of integration.

docs.title: 2. Definite integrals and areas
docs.text: Evaluate definite integrals and interpret them as signed area under a curve.

docs.title: Quiz
docs.text:
When you have been through the videos, test your understanding with the quiz below. The quiz contains 10 questions. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding. You can see your total score at the bottom of the page, as well as your score for each question. You can retake the quiz as many times as you like — results are not stored or assessed.

Question 1What is $\int 2x \, dx$?$2x^2 + C$$x^2 + C$$x^2$$2 + C$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: An antiderivative of $2x$ is $x^2$, so $\int 2x \, dx = x^2 + C$.

Question 2If $F'(x) = f(x)$, what do we call $F$?A derivative of $f$An antiderivative of $f$The slope of $f$The definite integral of $f$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: An antiderivative (or indefinite integral) of $f$ is a function $F$ whose derivative equals $f$.

Question 3Why do we write $+C$ after an indefinite integral?Because every integral equals zeroBecause antiderivatives are only unique up to an additive constantBecause $C$ is always the value at $x = 0$Because the integral sign requires it for notation onlyCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: If $F'(x) = f(x)$, then so does $(F + C)'(x)$ for any constant $C$. The $+C$ captures every possible antiderivative.

Question 4What is $\int x^4 \, dx$?$4x^3 + C$$\dfrac{x^5}{5} + C$$5x^4 + C$$\dfrac{x^4}{4} + C$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Reverse the power rule: $\int x^n \, dx = \dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C$ for $n \neq -1$, so $\int x^4 \, dx = \dfrac{x^5}{5} + C$.

Question 5What is $\int \dfrac{1}{x} \, dx$?$x^{-2} + C$$\ln|x| + C$$e^x + C$$-\dfrac{1}{x^2} + C$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The antiderivative of $\dfrac{1}{x}$ is the natural logarithm: $\int \dfrac{1}{x} \, dx = \ln|x| + C$ (for $x \neq 0$).

Question 6What is $\int \cos x \, dx$?$-\sin x + C$$\sin x + C$$\cos x + C$$-\cos x + C$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Since $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = \cos x$, we have $\int \cos x \, dx = \sin x + C$.

Question 7If $F'(x) = f(x)$, how is the definite integral $\displaystyle\int_a^b f(x)\, dx$ evaluated?$F(a) - F(b)$$F(b) - F(a)$$f(b) - f(a)$$F(b) + F(a)$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus gives $\displaystyle\int_a^b f(x)\, dx = F(b) - F(a)$, where $F$ is any antiderivative of $f$.

Question 8What is $\displaystyle\int_0^3 2x \, dx$?$6$$9$$3$$18$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: An antiderivative of $2x$ is $x^2$, so $\displaystyle\int_0^3 2x \, dx = [x^2]_0^3 = 9 - 0 = 9$.

Question 9When a function is below the $x$-axis on an interval, how does the definite integral relate to the geometric area?The integral equals the area exactlyThe integral is negative, giving a signed areaThe integral is always zeroThe integral cannot be computedCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Definite integrals measure signed area: regions above the axis contribute positively and regions below contribute negatively.

Question 10What is $\int (3x^2 - 4x + 1) \, dx$?$6x - 4 + C$$x^3 - 2x^2 + x + C$$x^3 - 2x^2 + C$$3x^3 - 4x^2 + x + C$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Integrate term by term: $\int 3x^2 \, dx = x^3$, $\int -4x \, dx = -2x^2$, and $\int 1 \, dx = x$, giving $x^3 - 2x^2 + x + C$.

docs.title: Exercise 3
docs.text:
Evaluate \(\int \dfrac{2}{x} \, dx\).

\(2\ln|x| + C\)

docs.title: Exercise 4
docs.text:
Evaluate \(\int (3\cos x - \sin x) \, dx\).

\(3\sin x + \cos x + C\)

docs.title: Exercise 5
docs.text:
Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_1^4 (2x + 1) \, dx\).

An antiderivative is \(x^2 + x\), so \([x^2 + x]_1^4 = (16 + 4) - (1 + 1) = 18\).

docs.title: Exercise 6
docs.text:
Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2} \sin x \, dx\).

\([-\cos x]_0^{\pi/2} = (-\cos(\pi/2)) - (-\cos 0) = 0 - (-1) = 1\).

docs.title: Exercise 7
docs.text:
Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_{-1}^1 x \, dx\) and explain the result in terms of signed area.

\(\left[\dfrac{x^2}{2}\right]_{-1}^1 = \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{1}{2} = 0\). The graph of \(y = x\) lies below the axis on \([-1, 0]\) and above on \([0, 1]\); equal areas with opposite signs cancel.

docs.title: Exercise 8
docs.text:
Find the area between the graph of \(f(x) = x^2\) and the \(x\)-axis from \(x = 0\) to \(x = 3\).

\(\displaystyle\int_0^3 x^2 \, dx = \left[\dfrac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^3 = 9\). Since \(x^2 \geq 0\) on \([0, 3]\), the integral equals the geometric area.

docs.title: Exercise 9
docs.text:
Verify that \(F(x) = x^3 + 2x\) is an antiderivative of \(f(x) = 3x^2 + 2\) by differentiating \(F\), then evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_0^1 (3x^2 + 2) \, dx\).

\(F'(x) = 3x^2 + 2 = f(x)\), so \(F\) is an antiderivative. \(\displaystyle\int_0^1 (3x^2 + 2) \, dx = F(1) - F(0) = (1 + 2) - 0 = 3\).

docs.title: Exercise 10
docs.text:
A velocity function is \(v(t) = 3t^2 - 2\) (in m/s). Find the displacement from \(t = 0\) to \(t = 2\) seconds.

Displacement \(= \displaystyle\int_0^2 (3t^2 - 2) \, dt = [t^3 - 2t]_0^2 = (8 - 4) - 0 = 4\) m.

docs.title: FAQ
docs.text: FAQ

docs.title: General information
docs.text:
What is Math Kickstarter?

Math Kickstarter is a self-paced online brush-up course covering foundational mathematics topics for engineering students at VIA University College. It includes short videos, self-check quizzes, and exercises with solutions.

Who should use this site?

This site is intended for students who scored below 75% on the mathematics pre-test. If you scored 75% or above, you generally do not need to work through this material.

Do I need to complete every topic?

No. Use the pre-test results to identify which topic areas you should review. Focus on those topics rather than working through the entire site from start to finish.

What topics are covered?

The site covers five areas: Basic Arithmetic and Equations, Functions, Differential Calculus, Integral Calculus, and Vectors in 2D. See the Topics overview for details.

Are advanced calculus techniques included?

No. This is a brush-up course. Topics such as the product, quotient, and chain rules for differentiation, and integration by substitution or partial fractions, are deliberately excluded.

docs.title: Using the site
docs.text:
In what order should I watch the videos?

Within each topic, watch the videos in the order they appear on the page. If you need several topics, we recommend starting with Basic Arithmetic and Equations and progressing through to Vectors in 2D.

What does a quiz score of 7/10 mean?

The quizzes are for self-assessment only. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests you have sufficient understanding of the material in that topic. Results are not stored or reported.

Where are the exercise solutions?

Solutions are available on each topic page. Click the answer blocks to reveal the solution.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text:
Who should I contact if I have questions about the material?

Contact Assistant Professor Mette Bjerg, memo@via.dk, for questions about the course content.

Who should I contact about the pre-test?

Contact Associate Professor Richard Brooks, rib@via.dk, for questions about the pre-test.

Who should I contact about my specific programme requirements?

Contact your programme coordinator for questions about your specific programme requirements.

docs.title: Tutorials
docs.text:
Tutorials

Step-by-step tutorials organised by topic. Use the table of contents to jump to a section, or scroll through the five main course areas below.

Each section matches one of the main topics on the site. Subsections correspond to specific skills or video themes — not every subsection will have tutorial material at launch. Return to the topic pages for videos, quizzes, and exercises.

docs.title: Basic Arithmetic and Equations
docs.text: Work through these tutorials after watching the Basic Arithmetic and Equations videos. Each subsection walks through the main ideas step by step, with worked examples similar to the quiz and exercises on that topic page.

docs.title: 1. Order of operations
docs.text:
When an expression contains several operations, use a fixed priority order (often remembered as PEMDAS):

  1. Parentheses ( ) — work inside brackets first
  2. Exponentiation — powers such as \(5^2\)
  3. Multiplication and division — from left to right
  4. Addition and subtraction — from left to right

A fraction bar groups the numerator and denominator as single expressions. Evaluate the numerator and denominator separately before dividing.

Example 1: Evaluate \(6 - 2 \times 5^2\).

  1. Exponent: \(5^2 = 25\)
  2. Multiplication: \(2 \times 25 = 50\)
  3. Subtraction: \(6 - 50 = -44\)

Example 2: Evaluate \(\dfrac{3 + 2 \times 6}{5}\).

  1. Inside the numerator, multiply first: \(2 \times 6 = 12\)
  2. Finish the numerator: \(3 + 12 = 15\)
  3. Divide: \(\dfrac{15}{5} = 3\)

Example 3: Evaluate \((6 - 2 \times 5)^2\).

  1. Inside the parentheses: \(6 - 10 = -4\)
  2. Square: \((-4)^2 = 16\)

Example 4: Evaluate \(24 \div 4 \times 3\).

Multiplication and division have equal priority, so work left to right: \(24 \div 4 = 6\), then \(6 \times 3 = 18\).

Check your understanding

Try Exercise 1 on the topic page.

docs.title: 2. Fractions
docs.text:
A fraction \(\dfrac{a}{b}\) has numerator \(a\) (top) and denominator \(b\) (bottom). The denominator tells you into how many equal parts a whole is divided.

Expand or reduce by multiplying or dividing numerator and denominator by the same non-zero number:

\[\dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{k \cdot a}{k \cdot b}\]

Adding or subtracting — denominators must match. If they do not, find a common denominator (often the least common multiple):

\[\dfrac{a}{b} + \dfrac{c}{d} = \dfrac{ad + bc}{bd}\]

Multiplying — multiply numerators and multiply denominators:

\[\dfrac{a}{b} \cdot \dfrac{c}{d} = \dfrac{ac}{bd}\]

Dividing — multiply by the reciprocal of the second fraction:

\[\dfrac{a}{b} \div \dfrac{c}{d} = \dfrac{a}{b} \cdot \dfrac{d}{c} = \dfrac{ad}{bc}\]

Worked example: Simplify \(\dfrac{5}{6} - \dfrac{1}{4}\).

  1. Common denominator of 6 and 4 is 12.
  2. \(\dfrac{5}{6} = \dfrac{10}{12}\) and \(\dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{3}{12}\)
  3. \(\dfrac{10}{12} - \dfrac{3}{12} = \dfrac{7}{12}\)

Worked example: Simplify \(\dfrac{2}{3} \div \dfrac{5}{9}\).

\[\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{9}{5} = \dfrac{18}{15} = \dfrac{6}{5}\] Check your understanding

Try Exercises 2 and 9 on the topic page.

docs.title: 3. Exponents and radicals
docs.text:
Powers: \(a^n\) means \(a\) multiplied by itself \(n\) times (\(n > 0\)).

Laws of exponents (same base \(a \neq 0\) where needed):

Rule Formula Product \(a^n \cdot a^m = a^{n+m}\) Quotient \(\dfrac{a^n}{a^m} = a^{n-m}\) Power of a power \((a^n)^m = a^{nm}\) Power of a product \((a \cdot b)^n = a^n b^n\) Power of a quotient \(\left(\dfrac{a}{b}\right)^n = \dfrac{a^n}{b^n}\) Negative exponent \(a^{-n} = \dfrac{1}{a^n}\) Zero exponent \(a^0 = 1\)

Square roots: if \(y^2 = x\), then \(y = \pm\sqrt{x}\). The symbol \(\sqrt{x}\) usually denotes the non-negative square root.

Worked example: Simplify \(\dfrac{2^4 \times 2^{-2}}{2^3}\).

  1. Numerator: \(2^{4+(-2)} = 2^2\)
  2. Quotient: \(\dfrac{2^2}{2^3} = 2^{2-3} = 2^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\)

Worked example: Simplify \(\dfrac{x^3 \cdot x^{-5}}{x^{-2}}\).

\[\dfrac{x^{-2}}{x^{-2}} = x^0 = 1\]

Worked example: Evaluate \(27^{\frac{1}{3}}\).

This is the cube root of 27, so the answer is \(3\).

Check your understanding

Try Exercise 3 on the topic page.

docs.title: 4. Parentheses
docs.text:
Distributive law — multiply each term inside the bracket:

\[a(b + c) = ab + ac\]

Adding a bracket — the signs stay the same:

\[2r + (3 - r) = 2r + 3 - r = r + 3\]

Subtracting a bracket — change the sign of every term inside:

\[2r - (3 - r) = 2r - 3 + r = 3r - 3\]

Special products (learn these patterns):

Pattern Expansion \((a + b)^2\) \(a^2 + 2ab + b^2\) \((a - b)^2\) \(a^2 - 2ab + b^2\) \((a + b)(a - b)\) \(a^2 - b^2\)

Worked example: Expand \(3x + 2(x - 7)\).

\[3x + 2x - 14 = 5x - 14\]

Worked example: Expand \((2 + 3x)^2\).

\[(2 + 3x)^2 = 4 + 12x + 9x^2 = 9x^2 + 12x + 4\] Check your understanding

Try Exercise 4 on the topic page.

docs.title: 5. Equations
docs.text:
The equals sign means the left-hand side and the right-hand side have the same value. It is not a signal to “write the answer next” — it states a balance.

Rules for solving:

  • Perform the same operation on both sides of the equation
  • You may simplify either side, as long as its value does not change
  • Aim to collect variable terms on one side and numbers on the other

Worked example: Solve \(4x - 2 = 18 - x\).

  1. Add \(x\) to both sides: \(5x - 2 = 18\)
  2. Add 2 to both sides: \(5x = 20\)
  3. Divide by 5: \(x = 4\)

Worked example: Solve \(\dfrac{2z + 3}{2} = z + 4\).

  1. Multiply both sides by 2: \(2z + 3 = 2z + 8\)
  2. Subtract \(2z\) from both sides: \(3 = 8\)

This is never true, so there is no solution.

Worked example: Solve \(\dfrac{3(x - 2)}{4} - \dfrac{2(x + 1)}{3} = 1\).

  1. Multiply through by 12 (LCM of 4 and 3): \(9(x - 2) - 8(x + 1) = 12\)
  2. Expand: \(9x - 18 - 8x - 8 = 12\)
  3. Simplify: \(x - 26 = 12\), so \(x = 38\)

Substituting back confirms the solution.

Check your understanding

Try Exercises 5 and 10 on the topic page.

docs.title: 6. Quadratic equations
docs.text:
A quadratic equation in standard form is

\[ax^2 + bx + c = 0 \quad (a \neq 0)\]

Before using the quadratic formula, expand and simplify until the equation is in this form.

Quadratic formula:

\[x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}\]

The discriminant is \(\Delta = b^2 - 4ac\):

Discriminant Real solutions \(\Delta > 0\) Two distinct real solutions \(\Delta = 0\) One repeated real solution \(\Delta < 0\) No real solutions

Worked example: Solve \(2x^2 - 4x - 6 = 0\).

  1. Divide by 2: \(x^2 - 2x - 3 = 0\)
  2. Factor: \((x - 3)(x + 1) = 0\)
  3. Solutions: \(x = 3\) or \(x = -1\)

Worked example: Solve \(4x(x + 1) + 3 = 2\).

  1. Expand: \(4x^2 + 4x + 3 = 2\)
  2. Standard form: \(4x^2 + 4x + 1 = 0\)
  3. Factor: \((2x + 1)^2 = 0\), so \(x = -\dfrac{1}{2}\) (one repeated root)

Worked example: For \(x^2 + 6x + 9 = 0\), the discriminant is \(36 - 36 = 0\), so there is one real solution. For \(3x^2 - 2x + 5 = 0\), \(\Delta = 4 - 60 = -56\), so there are no real solutions.

Check your understanding

Try Exercises 6 and 7 on the topic page.

docs.title: 7. Rearranging formulae
docs.text:
To make a variable the subject of a formula, treat the formula like an equation and undo operations in reverse order (inverse operations).

Strategy:

  1. Identify the structure: sums, products, powers, and parentheses
  2. Decide which variable to isolate
  3. Undo operations starting with the one furthest from that variable
  4. Apply the same operation to both sides at each step

Worked example: Make \(r\) the subject of \(V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3\).

  1. Divide both sides by \(\dfrac{4}{3}\pi\): \(r^3 = \dfrac{3V}{4\pi}\)
  2. Take the cube root: \(r = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3V}{4\pi}}\)

Worked example: Make \(r\) the subject of \(A = P(1 + r)^t\).

  1. Divide by \(P\): \(\dfrac{A}{P} = (1 + r)^t\)
  2. Take the \(t\)th root: \(1 + r = \left(\dfrac{A}{P}\right)^{1/t}\)
  3. Subtract 1: \(r = \left(\dfrac{A}{P}\right)^{1/t} - 1\)

Key idea: rearranging uses the same balance rules as solving equations — only the variable you want to isolate is treated as unknown; all other letters are constants for that step.

Check your understanding

Try Exercise 8 on the topic page.

docs.title: Vectors in 2D
docs.text:
Vectors in 2D

Work through the videos below in order. When you have watched them, take the quiz and try the exercises. A quiz score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
After working through this topic, you should be able to:

  • Represent vectors in the coordinate plane and perform vector algebra
  • Compute the dot product of two vectors and interpret the result
  • Use parametric equations to describe a line in 2D (optional)

docs.title: 1. Vectors in 2D
docs.text: Distinguish vectors from scalars, write vectors in component form, and add, subtract, and scale them.

docs.title: 2. Dot product
docs.text: Compute the dot product, find vector length, and determine angles between vectors.

docs.title: 3. Parametric equation of a line (optional)
docs.text: Describe a line in the plane using a position vector, a direction vector, and a parameter.

docs.title: Quiz
docs.text:
When you have been through the videos, test your understanding with the quiz below. The quiz contains 10 questions. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding. You can see your total score at the bottom of the page, as well as your score for each question. You can retake the quiz as many times as you like — results are not stored or assessed.

Question 1Which of the following is a vector quantity?SpeedDisplacementMassTemperatureCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: A vector has both magnitude and direction. Displacement specifies how far and in which direction something moves; speed, mass, and temperature are scalars (magnitude only).

Question 2Which notation correctly represents a vector with horizontal component $3$ and vertical component $-2$?$3 - 2$$(3, -2)$$|3, -2|$$3 \times (-2)$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: In component notation, a 2D vector is written as an ordered pair $(x, y)$ giving the horizontal and vertical components.

Question 3If $\mathbf{u} = (1, 2)$ and $\mathbf{v} = (4, -1)$, what is $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}$?$(5, 3)$$(5, 1)$$(4, 2)$$(1, -1)$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Add components: $(1 + 4,\; 2 + (-1)) = (5, 1)$.

Question 4If $\mathbf{u} = (5, 3)$ and $\mathbf{v} = (2, 1)$, what is $\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{v}$?$(7, 4)$$(3, 2)$$(3, 4)$$(2, 2)$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Subtract components: $(5 - 2,\; 3 - 1) = (3, 2)$.

Question 5What is $3\mathbf{a}$ when $\mathbf{a} = (2, -4)$?$(5, -1)$$(6, -12)$$(6, -4)$$(2, -12)$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Multiply each component by the scalar: $3(2, -4) = (6, -12)$.

Question 6What is the dot product $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}$ for $\mathbf{a} = (2, 3)$ and $\mathbf{b} = (4, 1)$?811145Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = a_x b_x + a_y b_y = 2 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 1 = 8 + 3 = 11$.

Question 7What is the length $|\mathbf{a}|$ of $\mathbf{a} = (3, 4)$?$7$$5$$25$$\sqrt{7}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: $|\mathbf{a}| = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$.

Question 8If $\theta$ is the angle between nonzero vectors $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$, which expression equals $\cos\theta$?$\dfrac{|\mathbf{a}| |\mathbf{b}|}{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}$$\dfrac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{|\mathbf{a}| |\mathbf{b}|}$$\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}$$\dfrac{|\mathbf{a}| + |\mathbf{b}|}{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The dot product formula is $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = |\mathbf{a}| |\mathbf{b}| \cos\theta$, so $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{|\mathbf{a}| |\mathbf{b}|}$.

Question 9Vectors $\mathbf{u} = (1, 2)$ and $\mathbf{v} = (4, -2)$ are perpendicular. Why?Because $|\mathbf{u}| = |\mathbf{v}|$Because $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$Because their components are equalBecause $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Two nonzero vectors are perpendicular when their dot product is zero: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = 1 \cdot 4 + 2 \cdot (-2) = 4 - 4 = 0$.

Question 10A line is given by $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{r}_0 + t\mathbf{d}$ with $\mathbf{r}_0 = (1, 2)$ and $\mathbf{d} = (3, 1)$. What is the position vector when $t = 2$?$(4, 3)$$(7, 4)$$(5, 3)$$(6, 2)$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: $\mathbf{r} = (1, 2) + 2(3, 1) = (1 + 6,\; 2 + 2) = (7, 4)$.

docs.title: Exercise 1
docs.text:
Given \(\mathbf{u} = (2, 1)\) and \(\mathbf{v} = (3, 4)\), find:

  1. \(\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}\)
  2. \(\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{v}\)
  3. \(2\mathbf{u}\)
  1. \((5, 5)\)
  2. \((-1, -3)\)
  3. \((4, 2)\)

docs.title: Exercise 2
docs.text:
Given \(\mathbf{a} = (1, 3)\) and \(\mathbf{b} = (2, -1)\), compute \(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}\).

\(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = 1 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot (-1) = 2 - 3 = -1\)

docs.title: Exercise 3
docs.text:
Find the length \(|\mathbf{c}|\) when \(\mathbf{c} = (-5, 12)\).

\(|\mathbf{c}| = \sqrt{(-5)^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13\)

docs.title: Exercise 4
docs.text:
Find \(\cos\theta\) for the angle \(\theta\) between \(\mathbf{a} = (1, 0)\) and \(\mathbf{b} = (1, 1)\).

\(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = 1\), \(|\mathbf{a}| = 1\), and \(|\mathbf{b}| = \sqrt{2}\), so \(\cos\theta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\)

docs.title: Exercise 5
docs.text:
Are \(\mathbf{u} = (2, 3)\) and \(\mathbf{v} = (3, -2)\) perpendicular? Justify your answer.

Yes. \(\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = 2 \cdot 3 + 3 \cdot (-2) = 6 - 6 = 0\), so the vectors are perpendicular.

docs.title: Exercise 6
docs.text:
Find the value of \(k\) such that \(\mathbf{a} = (1, k)\) and \(\mathbf{b} = (2, 4)\) satisfy \(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = 10\).

\(1 \cdot 2 + k \cdot 4 = 10 \;\Rightarrow\; 2 + 4k = 10 \;\Rightarrow\; 4k = 8 \;\Rightarrow\; k = 2\)

docs.title: Exercise 8
docs.text:
A line passes through the origin with direction vector \(\mathbf{d} = (2, 3)\). Using \(\mathbf{r} = t\mathbf{d}\), find the point on the line when \(t = 2\).

\(\mathbf{r} = 2(2, 3) = (4, 6)\)

docs.title: Exercise 9 (optional)
docs.text:
A line is given by \(\mathbf{r} = (1, 2) + t(1, -1)\). Find the position vector when \(t = 3\).

\(\mathbf{r} = (1, 2) + 3(1, -1) = (1 + 3,\; 2 - 3) = (4, -1)\)

docs.title: Exercise 10
docs.text:
Given \(\mathbf{a} = (1, 2)\) and \(\mathbf{b} = (3, 1)\), find \(|\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b}|\).

\(\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b} = (4, 3)\), so \(|\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b}| = \sqrt{4^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = 5\)

Total Score Summary

Your total score for all quizzes on this page is: 0 / 0
